# Anthropic’s Post
[![View organization page for Anthropic]()](https://www.linkedin.com/company/anthropicresearch?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)
[Anthropic](https://www.linkedin.com/company/anthropicresearch?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)
1,043,528 followers
7hEdited
Our CEO Dario Amodei on why a 10-year AI moratorium without federal backup is too blunt an instrument: "A.I. is advancing too head-spinningly fast. I believe that these systems could change the world, fundamentally, within two years; in 10 years, all bets are off. Without a clear plan for a federal response, a moratorium would give us the worst of both worlds —no ability for states to act, and no national policy as a backstop."
Read the full Guest Essay in the New York Times here:[https://lnkd.in/dgb7tV52](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/dgb7tV52&urlhash=NGiq&trk=public_post-text)

# Dario Amodei on Culture's Role in Leadership
This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Jace Malz
INBO•6K followers
2w
Dario Amodei said that he spends up to 40% of his time on culture at Anthropic. Not AI models. Not product strategy. Culture. His reasoning is that in the long run, it's the only thing that actually determines which company wins. I've been sitting with this all week. Most of the leaders I work with have very little time for this kind of work. But I think Amodei is pointing at something important about where leadership is heading. As AI takes on more of the execution, the distinctly human work like building trust, having honest conversations, getting clear on what actually matters becomes a top priority. I've spent the last year working alongside a few teams doing this slowly and intentionally. Meeting after meeting, honest conversation after honest conversation, getting clear on what's most important to them and how to work together to guide their organizations forward. It's the work I find most meaningful. And I have some capacity right now. If you're a leader sitting with a team challenge like trust, communication, a team that's technically functional but not really working together, I'd genuinely love to hear what's going on. Not to pitch you anything. Just to talk. You can reach me at jace@inbo.ca, or find me at inbo.ca.

Sona Jepsen
Pink Dragon•6K followers
1w
So what does emotional capacity actually mean in leadership? It’s not softness. It’s not therapy language. It’s not crying in meetings. It’s this: The ability to experience pressure without transmitting panic. That’s it. And you can see it clearly inside real companies. A board challenges the forecast. The CEO stays curious instead of defensive. A top performer pushes back. The CRO asks a better question instead of asserting dominance. A product launch misses. The team investigates patterns instead of searching for a scapegoat. Emotional capacity is the gap between stimulus and reaction. And that gap determines everything. → Whether feedback improves performance or erodes trust → Whether conflict sharpens strategy or fractures teams → Whether scale builds cohesion or chaos The companies that outperform don’t just have better strategy. They have leaders who can: → Hear hard truths without collapsing → Make decisions without theatrics → Hold ambiguity without rushing to false certainty → Regulate their own fear so others don’t have to That is the real compounding advantage. In the age of AI, as information accelerates, emotions accelerate too. And companies inevitably reflect the emotional ceiling of the leadership team. You cannot scale past that ceiling. Full stop. So if growth feels chaotic… reactive… or heavier than it should… It is rarely just a market problem. It is usually a capacity problem. This is where my work sits. At the intersection of human systems and commercial performance. When leaders expand their emotional capacity, organizations stabilize. When the emotional ceiling moves, the revenue ceiling tends to move with it. If you are leading through growth or AI-driven change and want to strengthen the human systems behind performance, feel free to reach out.

Leadership is still about human connection. This week’s Tuesday Reading, by Dr. Christian Schmieder, explores an important leadership reality: even as AI becomes more prevalent, the heart of effective leadership remains human relationships. Technology can help us work faster and make better decisions, but it does not replace the essential, consistent human interactions that build trust, collaboration, and belonging. In environments shaped by digital tools and AI support, leaders must be intentional about sustaining meaningful connections with others. That requires: • Creating space to listen with presence • Prioritizing authentic conversation • Asking questions (and actively listening) to deepen understanding • Remaining emotionally available, not emotionally distant The leaders who thrive with AI are not the ones who automate every task. They are the ones who use technology to free up time for what only humans can do well. They focus their energy on trust, empathy, inclusion, and support. As you think about your own leadership practice this week, consider this: what relationship in your work would benefit from more intentional presence and connection? Read Christian's perspectives at https://lnkd.in/drw68HQ3#LeadershipDevelopment#LeadershipPresence#EmployeeEngagement#LeadershipPractices#MORLeaders

🔥 I’ll tell you the exact moment leadership breaks… and it’s not the moment you think. 👇 (If you’ve ever felt yourself tightening under pressure, this one’s going to land.) 🧠 I used to think leadership cracked in the big, dramatic moments. The crisis. The tough call. The room where everyone turns to you. But that’s not where it happens. Leadership breaks in the quiet places, the moments your nervous system gets stretched past what it can hold. ⚠️ It breaks the moment you start tightening instead of trusting. ⚠️ It breaks when you avoid the conversation you know you need to have. ⚠️ It breaks when you perform confidence instead of asking the real question. ⚠️ It breaks when you take something personally that was never about you. None of that looks dramatic from the outside. But inside a team? It changes everything. 💬 People stop telling the truth early. 🧩 Decisions get heavier than they need to be. ⏳ Execution slows because everyone is managing you instead of the work. And here’s the part leaders rarely see until it’s too late: Your team isn’t reacting to the situation. They’re reacting to your regulation. 🧘♂️ When you’re steady, the room settles. 🧭 When you wobble, the room compensates. 🤝 When you stay human under pressure, people follow without needing to be convinced. Tools don’t fix that. Strategy doesn’t fix that. Titles definitely don’t fix that. Capacity does. The internal kind. ✨ The kind you build quietly. ✨ The kind no one applauds. ✨ The kind that holds everything together because you did. If you’ve ever felt yourself tightening in those small moments, you’re not broken. You’re just hitting the edge of your current operating system. And edges? They’re invitations. 🔁 Repost to help another leader find their edge 🔔 Follow Zach Mural PhD for the human‑intelligence layer of leadership and AI adoption

For the past two weeks I’ve been heads down on something. Late nights. Early mornings. A lot of thinking about growth, leadership, and how AI is changing the way we work. It felt like the right moment to pause and write down a few lessons that kept coming up during this time. Here are the five that stood out: 1. Always believe in yourself (unless you’re AI) 2. Surround yourself with great people (like me) 3. Stay curious (without surveillance) 4. When in doubt, add a numbered list to make things look more insightful 5. If you mention AI somewhere in the post, engagement increases by at least 30% Full transparency though. None of this happened. There was no project. No two-week journey. No late nights or reflection. I spent about two minutes writing two prompts asking AI to generate a LinkedIn post about a meaningful two-week experience that never existed, formatted in a way that looks like something worth your time. If you made it this far, please like, comment, and follow for more leadership insights.

Everyone's optimizing. Nobody's being honest. Leaders right now are being asked to do more with less, transform the org, and keep exhausted people engaged. All at once. Most are coping by toeing the line: AI will bring more creativity, more breathing room, more innovation. Employees know that's not true. Research is starting to confirm it. So when you're the leader saying "AI will make your life better" while every lived experience on your team says the opposite, you're not just wrong. You're eroding the one thing that actually holds a team together right now. Trust. The leaders who win this moment aren't the ones with the best strategy deck. They're the ones willing to say: this is genuinely hard, I see it, and I'm not going to pretend otherwise. That honesty doesn't make you weak. It makes you the signal in a room full of noise.

It feels meaningful to finally share something that began as a quiet question twenty-one years ago. In 2005, I began an inquiry that has evolved throughout my work with leaders: How do we recognize when our work, our decisions, and our lives are truly aligned with who we are? Over the years, that question showed up in conversations, coaching, leadership programs, and moments of reflection with extraordinary people navigating complex decisions. Slowly, something began to take shape: An understanding that alignment itself requires a form of intelligence we rarely name. More recently, that insight resurfaced as I began thinking about AI … Not Artificial Intelligence. Alignment Intelligence The capacity to sense when something is true for you, and when it is not. The abiliry to notice the subtle moment when your words begin to drift away from what you actually believe. The awareness that arises when a decision may look successful on the outside yet feels quietly misaligned on the inside. In my work with leaders, I see something interesting. The most effective leaders are not simply those with the most information. They are those with the highest Alignment Intelligence. They can sense when a strategy is technically correct but humanly disconnected. They can feel when a conversation is avoiding the truth that would actually move things forward. They can recognize when success is beginning to drift away from authenticity. This form of intelligence cannot be automated. It emerges through reflection, attention, and the willingness to listen to the deeper signals that surface when we slow down long enough to notice them. I am so excited to share that I have finally brought these years of inquiry and experience into form as a reflection tool called the Authentic Alignment Compass, a short self-assessment designed to help leaders pause and explore where alignment is present in their work and lives, and where something may be asking for attention. After holding this question for more than two decades, it feels meaningful to finally bring it into form. I feel grateful to begin sharing it with you. You can explore it here: https://lnkd.in/e6vEcXae

Michael Steele

Most strategies don’t fail because the plan was wrong. They fail because the organization still believes the old story. A company announces it wants to become more innovative. But managers still believe experimentation is risky. A leadership team commits to adopting AI. But employees still believe AI threatens their jobs. An organization says it wants faster decision-making. But leaders still believe every choice requires consensus. The strategy may be new. But the belief system is old. And belief systems are powerful. They shape how people interpret risk. They determine which ideas get traction. They influence what managers reward, question, or ignore. That’s why transformation is rarely just about new initiatives. It’s about changing the narrative inside the organization. The most effective leaders understand this. They reinforce new ideas consistently. They bring in outside perspectives that challenge assumptions. They repeat the message until it becomes the new normal. They leave environments where autonomy is low, emotional intelligence is absent, and they don’t feel seen as whole humans. The same traits that make us strong partners in life — clarity, accountability, curiosity, boundaries, empathy — are the traits that build high-trust teams. When leaders create autonomy: • People take ownership instead of waiting for permission. • Innovation expands instead of shrinking. • Performance becomes sustainable instead of performative. And perhaps most importantly: When leaders are internally aligned — professionally AND personally — teams feel it. Clarity creates confidence. Autonomy builds trust. And trust is where real performance lives. Who we are as humans doesn’t dilute our leadership. It defines it.

# Anthropic CEO Amodei on AI governance and oversight

Rubén Domínguez Ibar
3mo
JUST IN: Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei says he should not be in charge of AI’s future He admits he is “deeply uncomfortable” with how a small group of tech leaders is making decisions that will affect billions of people. The same people building the most powerful systems are also the ones defining the guardrails. There is still no clear governance model and no agreement on who should make these calls. What should a better structure for AI oversight look like?
51 Comments
Like Comment
Patrizia Bertini 3mo
This is probably the most positive development emerging from the AI landscape in quite some time! Because it's true: corporations, whose primary objective is profit over social good, cannot be trusted with something that carries massive social consequences. We consistently fail to recognise that AI is not merely a tool it has agency and decision-making embedded within it. It learns from our historical biases and replicates injustice based on the data it has been trained on. Dario Amodei is equally aware - a research from Anthropic demonstrates that a model requires only 250 files to become vulnerable to malicious use. Data poisoning attacks represent a critical vulnerability, and that is a significant oversight. IMHO we must examine the European governance approach more closely. The AI Act's deep focus on serving social good rather than corporate profit is key. Many EU regulations that corporations resent actually place human welfare at the heart of progress and innovation, viewing innovation as a means to serve human flourishing and social inclusion. This is what too many people fail to recognise: innovation is not a race for profit, but a pathway to positive social impact. A genuinely encouraging sign, in my view.

Most leaders vent. Few translate. 🧠⚡️ I know both sides because I’ve done both. Earlier in my career, I sometimes thought saying the problem clearly was enough. It is so easy to just say: “The team doesn’t understand the priorities.” “The meetings are too much.” “The M&A process is chaos.” “AI adoption is too slow.” But clarity without conversion is just better-formatted frustration. What changed for me was brutal and simple: Problem → what I did → what changed → what you can use 🔁 That pattern has saved me again and again. A few real examples from my world: In transactions, I learned that complaining about “lack of readiness” is lazy. The better move is to say: “We were too founder-dependent, so we built a weekly cash cadence, clearer ownership, and decision transparency 24 months before the process.” That is Fit-for-Transaction. Not a slogan. A habit. 🏗️ In AI, I learned that saying “people resist change” is incomplete. The better truth is: “Leaders were delegating understanding, so teams treated AI as optional. Once leaders started going hands-on themselves, adoption changed.” That is AI Meets EQ. Technology plus modeled behavior. 🤖❤️ In one company context, I watched meetings explode because nobody wanted to kill a bad rhythm. The fix was not another complaint about calendars. It was removing status theatre, forcing real decisions, and making visible what actually required a meeting in the first place. 📅✂️ That’s the difference: Venting says, “This is broken.” Value says, “Here is what I changed, what happened next, and what it might save you.” ✅ And in 2026, that matters even more. Because AI is creating a growing divide between people who know how to convert noise into usable judgment… and those who only add to the noise. 🌪️ Nobody needs more executive frustration. A lot of leaders are seeing the same pattern. The question I keep getting is straightforward. What do we do about it? This is truly a leadership problem and not a technology problem. 𝗖𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝘀𝗽𝗮𝗰𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗸 𝗯𝗲𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘂𝘀𝗲𝗱 Start with the question, not the tool. Ask people what they think before anything gets opened or checked. 𝟮. 𝗕𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝘄𝗵𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗯𝗼𝗮𝗿𝗱 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗱𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻 𝘀𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 Pull people out of their screens. Work problems together. Have them explain how they’re thinking. If they can’t explain it, they don’t understand it yet. 𝟯. 𝗨𝘀𝗲 𝘀𝗺𝗮𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗿, 𝗵𝘂𝗺𝗮𝗻-𝘀𝗰𝗮𝗹𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 Not every problem needs a large meeting. Put 4 to 6 people in a room, focus on one issue, and have a real conversation. 𝟰. 𝗕𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝗽𝗮𝘂𝘀𝗲 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗼 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻-𝗺𝗮𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 Speed matters, but judgment matters more. Create space before important decisions so people can actually think. 𝟱. 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗲𝗻𝗴𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝘀𝗲𝗹𝗳-𝗮𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗮𝗰𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 This starts with you. Where are you defaulting to tools instead of thinking? Where are you moving too fast? Your team will take its cues from you. This is the work. It’s not new tools or new systems. It’s how leaders choose to show up. Thinking doesn’t disappear all at once. The biggest myth in leadership and innovation and why it is holding you back. 🚩 Years ago I watched a talented team launch a “big idea” that failed not because the idea was bad, but because the environment around it was broken. Leadership cheered the concept, but never cleared decision rights, never protected learning time, and never made small failures safe. Culture is not an HR checkbox; it is a leadership muscle you must exercise daily. In Chapter 3 of my book I lay out the AIR framework, a practical leadership playbook for the AI era, and it is the heart of building an innovation culture. Three short ideas that change the game: - Agility: leaders must shrink feedback loops; reward fast experiments over polished plans. Create structures where teams can sense, try, and learn every sprint. - Intelligence: treat data and AI as partners, not magic substitutes. Leaders translate insight into decision clarity, they define when machines suggest and when humans decide. - Resilience: normalize recovery and learning, not perfection. Resilient teams recover faster, adapt policy faster, and sustain creativity under pressure. Trust underpins all three. Without psychological safety and clear accountability, AI amplifies fear, not creativity. My Trust Playbook shows how to codify boundaries between automation and human judgment, so people can innovate without losing control. Practical moves any leader can start this week: - Map decision rights, then remove the 1 or 2 blockers that slow every experiment. - Run a 2-week micro-experiment, measure learning not vanity metrics, share the playbook companywide. - Publish a failure journal, celebrate what was learned, and lock in one process improvement. - Use tools that make learning visible; the right app can track experiments, map decision owners, surface trust metrics, and run lightweight scenario simulations with AI. These features turn good intentions into repeatable routines. Leadership is not a title; it is the daily practice of shaping context. Strategy is not a static plan; it is the set of choices that create permission to experiment. Combine both and innovation becomes less accidental and more reliable. What is one small policy you could change today to make experimentation safer in your organisation? 🤔 Interested in the full framework?
